steps.title: Notice the sarcasm
steps.content: The narrator first gives Sir Winston Day the appearance of distinction: features fit for a bust or stamp and a forehead suggesting intelligence. Then the tone turns sarcastic, saying it would be impertinent to ask too closely about the actual achievements from his long public service.

steps.title: Describe the contrast
steps.content: A keeps both parts of the description: Day looks important, but the text leaves his accomplishments uncertain. B invents dishonorable actions in the memoirs. C treats the state-secrets joke as literal secrecy about achievements. D wrongly says he is modest and capable, even though the passage stresses impressive appearance and uncertainty about what he has actually achieved. The answer is A.
